a: High-mix prototype shops, HVAC duct fabricators, enclosures, and repeat OEM production have different task and automation profiles.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Job, project, or repeat-production quotes for custom sheet-metal parts and assemblies, often under recurring contractor and OEM accounts with material, engineering support, programming, fabrication, finishing, inspection, and delivery bundled.
lens.excluded_activities: On-site sheet-metal installation contractors, stampers and custom roll formers classified elsewhere, captive OEM departments, commodity distributors, firms outside the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market sheet-metal job shops and contract manufacturers repeatedly providing outsourced detailing, cutting, punching, bending, forming, welding, assembly, finishing, and delivery for external HVAC, construction, equipment, enclosure, industrial, and OEM customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader fabricated-metal occupation mix and all-industry sheet-metal workers rather than wage-weighted tasks in LMM NAICS 332322 manufacturers. The range maps high exposure in quoting, drawing review, takeoff, CAD/CAM, nesting, CNC programming, scheduling, purchasing, quality-image review, documentation, and customer communication against lower exposure in material handling, machine setup, forming, welding, assembly, grinding, finishing, maintenance, and inspection; all-industry sheet-metal data include field installers, likely understating factory automation.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
High-mix prototype shops, HVAC duct fabricators, enclosures, and repeat OEM production have different task and automation profiles.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across at least twenty-five LMM sheet-metal shops, split among estimating, engineering, CAD/CAM, programming, scheduling, procurement, laser and punch, brake, welding, assembly, finishing, quality, logistics, sales, and administration, with current automation documented.
a.rationale: BLS notes that some sheet-metal fabrication equipment is automated, while manufacturing workers still lift materials and stand for long periods. The broader fabricated-metal workforce remains concentrated in assemblers, welders, machinists, and machine operators, so the main AI opportunity surrounds production rather than eliminating most direct fabrication.

d5.bridge: BLS sheet-metal employment projections and Census construction value proxy portions of sheet-metal demand rather than constant-quality output of eligible LMM manufacturers. BLS includes installation, while construction omits many OEM and industrial parts and is nominal; prefabrication and outsourced fabrication share may allow manufacturing demand to outgrow field employment.
caveats: Occupation and construction measures do not directly measure physical outsourced sheet-metal output.
Tariffs, metal prices, interest rates, HVAC efficiency policy, OEM cycles, and reshoring can materially shift demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year unit and revenue forecasts by HVAC duct, enclosures, machinery, electronics, transportation, construction, and industrial segment, plus customer schedules, RFQ counts, backlog, cancellations, capacity utilization, import substitution, and shop shipments adjusted for metal prices.
d5.rationale: BLS projects sheet-metal worker employment up 2% from 2024 to 2034 and cites continuing demand for energy-efficient HVAC, while noting prefabricated duct and automated equipment reduce labor. Broad May 2026 construction was down 1.5% year over year with mixed submarkets. Five-year demand should be approximately stable to modestly higher across HVAC, enclosures, equipment, electronics, construction, and reshored OEM production, with cyclical downside.

caveats: Eligibility is not directly observed.
The provided count is margin-bridged and may include micro-shops, captive firms, or cyclically overearning businesses without transferable systems.
e.change_evidence: A screened census of the 1,292 estimated candidates covering external revenue, repeat-order share, contract-manufacturing scope, normalized EBITDA, owner dependence, customer concentration, certifications, equipment condition, working capital, claims, and environmental exposure.
e.rationale: Many sheet-metal manufacturers are outsourced job shops or contract manufacturers producing to repeat external customer specifications, making them service-like despite manufacturing classification. Eligibility is reduced by captive units, commodity sellers, one-customer shops, owner-held relationships, weak systems, obsolete equipment, excessive project concentration, and firms outside true normalized EBITDA limits.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $10,097.4M / sectoral output (T30) $35,251.9M, year 2023

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $308,648 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332322); class <5: 1,325 firms, avg revenue/firm $614,180 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $120,502 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 814 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,753,134 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $343,965 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 724 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,446,307 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $676,165 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,024 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,896,669 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,137,926 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 268 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,140,112 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,267,890 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 95 firms, avg revenue/firm $118,967,526 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $23,341,429 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,292

caveats: Accountable production may consolidate into larger platforms outside the lens.
Routine flat-part cutting is more susceptible to platform self-service than formed, welded, and finished assemblies.
o.change_evidence: OEM and contractor sourcing data on shifts to captive production, imported finished parts, digital manufacturing marketplaces, self-service instant quoting, and lights-out networks, plus certification, quality, warranty, and liability requirements.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires an accountable operator to interpret drawings and revisions, select material and processes, program and maintain equipment, form and join parts, inspect quality, coordinate finishing and delivery, and stand behind conformance. Software and automation can enable self-service ordering and lights-out cutting, but variable fabrication and assembly still require a responsible manufacturer.

caveats: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
Retention varies between transactional laser cutting, repeat OEM programs, and complex finished assemblies.
q.change_evidence: Matched quote, invoice, and actual-cost histories before and after automation, including metal escalators, labor assumptions, discounts, RFQ win rates, resourcing, scrap, rework, claims, lead time, volumes, and annual customer repricing.
q.rationale: Fixed quotes permit initial retention, especially for fast-turn, complex, low-volume, certified, or integrated assemblies. Competitive RFQs, annual OEM cost-downs, material escalators, alternative sourcing, and contractor bid pressure pass common productivity gains through. Faster response, lower scrap, shorter lead time, and fewer quality escapes should retain better than visible reductions in standard machine hours.

caveats: Observed equipment automation does not directly measure AI implementation.
Small shops may lack standardized routings and clean historical costs needed for reliable models.
rho.change_evidence: Two-year pre/post results from comparable shops deploying AI in quoting, DFM review, nesting, programming, scheduling, predictive maintenance, machine vision, documentation, and customer service, including labor, scrap, rework, throughput, lead time, and overrides.
rho.rationale: CAD files, historical quotes, routings, nests, machine logs, inspection images, schedules, and service records create implementable use cases in quoting, programming, planning, predictive maintenance, and quality. Constraints include inconsistent drawings, short runs, legacy ERP and equipment, cybersecurity, customer-specific tolerances, limited IT capacity, and the cost of bad bends, collisions, scrap, or late assemblies.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Discounting stated plans for closures, internal transfers, failed sales, and equipment-only liquidations yields the base; a fragmented mature job-shop base and technician retirements could make the general proxy conservative, while owner dependence and customer concentration can block transfer.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures plans, not completed transfers.
Equipment and customer accounts may be purchased without transfer of an operating firm.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of NAICS 332322 closed deals, asset sales, closures, and owner changes from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, manufacturing brokers, SBA loans, UCC records, and state registries, matched to firm eligibility and owner age.
s5.rationale: The large fragmented population should generate substantial succession and strategic activity, but only completed external going-concern control transfers qualify. Customer retention, management depth, working capital, equipment, certifications, and repeat program quality determine financeability.

principal_driver: A very large outsourced job-shop base repeats digital quoting and planning work ahead of every physical fabrication order.
principal_weakness: Competitive sourcing and a substantial physical workforce cap retained AI benefits.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily engaged in manufacturing sheet-metal work other than stampings and distinguishes on-site fabrication and adjacent product categories.
sources.title: NAICS 332322 Sheet Metal Work Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports 127,000 sheet-metal workers in 2024, 24% employed in manufacturing, 2% projected employment growth through 2034, continuing HVAC demand, and labor effects from prefabricated duct and automated fabrication equipment.
sources.title: Sheet Metal Workers

sources.supports: Reports 2025 broader-subsector employment including 125,250 assemblers, 112,520 welders, 101,640 machinists, and 56,060 cutting and press-machine operators.
sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332

sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 5% planned closure and 5% had no plan.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports May 2026 total construction at a seasonally adjusted $2.210 trillion annual rate, down 1.5% from May 2025, with mixed private and public submarkets.
sources.title: Value of Construction Put in Place, May 2026

title: Sheet Metal Work Manufacturing
